name: 5-AMINO 1 MQ
desc: An inhibitor of the enzyme nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), implicated in cellular energy expenditure.
contraindications: Concurrent high-dose niacin supplementation — may reduce inhibitory efficacy
Co-administration with PARP inhibitors — potential NAD+ pathway interference
Hepatic impairment — risk of incomplete metabolic clearance
Pregnancy or lactation — no safety data available
description: A selective small-molecule inhibitor of nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), an enzyme overexpressed in adipose tissue that diverts S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) from methyl cycle metabolism. NNMT inhibition elevates intracellular NAD+ and activates SIRT1/SIRT3 epigenetic regulation.
objective: Metabolic optimization, adipose tissue regulation, cellular energy enhancement

storage_conditions.container: Sealed amber vial or opaque HDPE bottle

pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic; CYP450 involvement not fully characterized

mechanisms: Selective competitive inhibition of nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT) enzyme activity
Restoration of intracellular NAD+ pools via methyl cycle redirection
Indirect SIRT1 and SIRT3 deacetylase activation through NAD+ substrate availability
Downregulation of adipogenesis transcription factors in white adipose tissue
safetyNote: For in vitro or animal research only. Not approved for human use. Handle with appropriate PPE. Potential mutagenic or teratogenic effects not fully evaluated. Dispose per institutional biohazard protocols.

name: AOD-9604
desc: A modified fragment of the C-terminal region of Human Growth Hormone noted for lipolytic stimulation.
safetyNote: Research peptide derived from HGH C-terminus. Does not activate IGF-1 axis. Generally well tolerated; injection site reactions possible. Not approved by FDA for therapeutic use.
mechanisms: Selective beta-3 adrenergic receptor stimulation in adipocytes
Lipolytic activity without IGF-1 axis activation
Prevention of diet-induced insulin resistance
Stimulation of fat oxidation via fatty acid mobilization

name: ARA-290
desc: A non-erythropoietic peptide engineered to interact with the innate repair receptor, reducing inflammation and neurodegeneration.
safetyNote: Investigational compound for neuropathic pain and inflammation. Well tolerated in Phase II trials. Avoid in active hematological malignancy (erythropoietin receptor activity).
mechanisms: Non-hematopoietic erythropoietin receptor (EPOR/βcR) agonism
Peripheral neuroprotection via JAK2/STAT3 signaling
Anti-inflammatory cytokine modulation (TNF-α, IL-6 suppression)
Promotion of small fiber nerve regeneration

name: BPC-157
desc: A pentadecapeptide with potent cytoprotective, angiogenic, and wound-healing properties under experimental conditions.
contraindications: Active malignancy or high cancer risk
safetyNote: Research peptide with extensive animal data. No approved human clinical trials completed. Use with caution in patients with history of malignancy due to angiogenic properties.

pharmacokinetics.bioavailability: SC/IM preferred; oral active in GI

mechanisms: Upregulation of growth hormone receptor expression in tendon fibroblasts
Nitric oxide (NO) pathway modulation promoting angiogenesis
FAK-paxillin pathway activation for wound healing
Cytoprotective gastric mucosa stabilization via PGE2 synthesis

aiContent.summary: A powerful combination of BPC-157 and TB-500 aimed at maximizing tissue repair, accelerating healing, and reducing systemic inflammation.
aiContent.beginnerExplanation: This blend combines BPC-157, known for healing tendons and the gut, with TB-500, which promotes muscle repair and cellular migration. Together, they offer a comprehensive approach to recovery from injuries.
aiContent.scientificSummary: The synergistic administration of BPC-157 and Thymosin Beta-4 (TB-500) targets multiple pathways of tissue regeneration. BPC-157 upregulates growth hormone receptors and promotes angiogenesis, while TB-500 acts as an actin-sequestering protein to facilitate cellular migration and tissue remodeling.

name: CJC-1295 with DAC
desc: A long-acting GHRH analog paired with a Drug Affinity Complex to maximize half-life and stimulate continuous GH release.
contraindications: Pediatric patients with open growth plates
safetyNote: Long-acting GHRH analog. Monitor IGF-1 levels to avoid supraphysiologic GH elevation. Potential for water retention and carpal tunnel at high doses. Weekly dosing protocol; avoid stacking with other GHRH analogs.
scientificName: CJC-1295 DAC; Drug Affinity Complex-modified GRF(1-29)
mechanisms: GHRH receptor agonism with drug affinity complex (DAC) albumin binding
Sustained pulsatile GH secretion from anterior pituitary somatotrophs
IGF-1 axis upregulation for anabolic and lipolytic effects

faqModalItems.q: How should CJC-1295 with DAC be categorized semantically?
faqModalItems.a: Around growth-hormone-axis research, secretagogue exploration, and performance or body-composition related contexts.

faqModalItems.a: Because they are different product families in practice and should not be merged in semantic or FAQ handling.

name: CJC-1295 without DAC (Modified GRF 1-29)
desc: A synthetic GHRH analog that increases pulsatile growth hormone and IGF-1 secretions without altering cortisol levels.
contraindications: Pediatric patients with open growth plates
safetyNote: Short-acting GHRH analog. Requires pulsatile dosing (2–3× daily). Monitor IGF-1. Lower risk of supraphysiologic GH accumulation vs. DAC version. Injection site reactions common.
mechanisms: GHRH receptor agonism without DAC modification
Short-burst GH secretion mimicking physiological pulses
IGF-1 axis stimulation for tissue repair and fat metabolism

aiContent.summary: A synergistic blend of a GHRH analogue and a GHRP designed to naturally stimulate endogenous growth hormone release.
aiContent.beginnerExplanation: This combination uses CJC-1295 to signal the brain to release growth hormone, and Ipamorelin to amplify this signal. It provides a natural, pulsatile release of GH for improved recovery, anti-aging, and metabolic benefits.
aiContent.scientificSummary: CJC-1295 without DAC acts as a Growth Hormone Releasing Hormone (GHRH) analogue, stimulating the pituitary, while Ipamorelin, a selective Growth Hormone Secretagogue (GHS), binds to the ghrelin receptor. The co-administration produces a synergistic, pulsatile release of endogenous GH without significantly elevating cortisol or prolactin.

typeData.halfLife: CJC-1295 w/o DAC: ~30 min. Ipamorelin: ~2 hours

peptide.mechanismOfAction: Shorter-acting growth-hormone-axis research peptide usually grouped with secretagogue and pulsatile signaling contexts.

name: CJC-1295 without DAC + Ipamorelin
desc: A synthetic GHRH analog that increases pulsatile growth hormone and IGF-1 secretions without altering cortisol levels.
contraindications: Pediatric patients with open growth plates
safetyNote: Short-acting GHRH analog. Requires pulsatile dosing (2–3× daily). Monitor IGF-1. Lower risk of supraphysiologic GH accumulation vs. DAC version. Injection site reactions common.
mechanisms: GHRH receptor agonism without DAC modification
Short-burst GH secretion mimicking physiological pulses
IGF-1 axis stimulation for tissue repair and fat metabolism

name: Cagrilintide
desc: A long-acting amylin analog investigated for its synergistic action in energy balance and satiation mechanisms.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma
safetyNote: Long-acting amylin analog under clinical investigation (CagriSema trials). Risk of nausea and vomiting especially on dose escalation. Thyroid C-cell monitoring may be warranted. Not approved for commercial use.
mechanisms: Long-acting amylin receptor agonism (AMY1, AMY2, AMY3)
Central satiety signaling via area postrema and NTS
Synergistic GLP-1 pathway potentiation for weight reduction
Delayed gastric emptying and reduced caloric intake

name: HCG
desc: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin; extensively utilized to stimulate the Leydig cells in the synthesis of endogenous testosterone.
safetyNote: Gonadotropin hormone. Risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in women. May promote benign and malignant androgen-sensitive conditions. Monitor testosterone and estradiol levels.
mechanisms: LH receptor (LHCGR) agonism in Leydig cells stimulating testosterone synthesis
FSH receptor cross-reactivity at high doses
Corpus luteum maintenance via progesterone induction
Spermatogenesis support via intratesticular testosterone elevation

name: HGH
desc: Recombinant Human Growth Hormone essential for systemic cellular regeneration, growth, and metabolic regulation.
contraindications: Active Prader-Willi syndrome with severe obesity
Epiphyseal closure not completed (inappropriate GH deficiency diagnosis)
safetyNote: Recombinant human growth hormone. Requires prescription. Risk of insulin resistance, edema, carpal tunnel, and intracranial hypertension. Monitor IGF-1, glucose, and thyroid function regularly.
mechanisms: Direct GH receptor (GHR) activation triggering JAK2/STAT5b signaling
Hepatic IGF-1 synthesis induction for systemic anabolic effects
Lipolysis via hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) upregulation
Protein synthesis enhancement via mTOR pathway activation

name: Hexarelin
desc: One of the most potent fast-acting GH secretagogues.

Cardioprotective action via CD36/ghrelin receptor in cardiomyocytes
Modest cortisol and prolactin co-secretion at high doses
IGF-1 axis activation for muscle hypertrophy and fat loss

name: IGF-1 LR3
desc: A highly engineered variant of Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 with an extended half-life dictating profound cellular hyperplasia.
safetyNote: Long-acting IGF-1 analog. Significant hypoglycemia risk — administer post-meal. Promotes cell proliferation; avoid in oncological history. Requires careful dosing titration.
scientificName: Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 Long Arg3; Long R3 IGF-1
mechanisms: Insulin-like growth factor receptor 1 (IGF-1R) agonism driving PI3K/AKT/mTOR anabolic cascade
Skeletal muscle satellite cell proliferation and differentiation
Anti-apoptotic signaling via Bcl-2/Bcl-xL upregulation
Reduced IGFBP binding compared to native IGF-1 extending bioactivity

name: Ipamorelin
desc: A highly selective and potent GHRP that stimulates massive growth hormone release safely without spiking cortisol or prolactin.

mechanisms: Highly selective GHS-R1a agonism without cortisol or prolactin co-secretion
Pulsatile GH release from anterior pituitary somatotrophs
IGF-1 axis stimulation for muscle anabolism and fat metabolism
No desensitization of GH axis at therapeutic doses

name: MT2 (Melanotan II)
desc: A synthetic analog of alpha-MSH inducing remarkable melanogenesis and demonstrating robust aphrodisiac properties.
contraindications: Personal or family history of melanoma
safetyNote: Melanotan II. Non-selective melanocortin agonist. Risk of nausea, spontaneous erections, and blood pressure changes. IMPORTANT: Associated with melanocyte activation — monitor nevi. Not FDA approved.
mechanisms: Non-selective melanocortin receptor (MC1R–MC5R) agonism
MC1R activation increasing eumelanin synthesis for UV-independent skin pigmentation
MC4R hypothalamic activation inducing sexual arousal and penile erection
Appetite suppression and lipolysis via MC3R/MC4R signaling

name: Semaglutide
desc: A selective GLP-1 receptor agonist with structural modifications to extend plasma half-life via albumin binding.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma
safetyNote: GLP-1 agonist (Ozempic/Wegovy). Risk of nausea, pancreatitis, and gallbladder disease. Thyroid C-cell hyperplasia in rodents — monitor in MTC risk patients. Requires gradual dose titration.

storage_conditions.temperature: 2–8°C (unused); room temp up to 28 days after first use

Glucose-dependent insulin secretion enhancement from pancreatic beta cells
Glucagon suppression during hyperglycemia
Central appetite suppression via hypothalamic GLP-1 receptors and vagal afferents

name: Tirzepatide
desc: A dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist fundamentally altering energy metabolism and insulin secretion profiles.
description: A once-weekly synthetic dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist (twincretin) developed by Eli Lilly. FDA-approved for T2DM (Mounjaro) and chronic weight management (Zepbound). Demonstrates superior glycemic and weight outcomes vs. GLP-1 monotherapy in the SURPASS trial series.
objective: Weight reduction, glycemic control, metabolic syndrome management, insulin sensitivity

storage_conditions.reconstitution: Ready to inject solution; no reconstitution required
storage_conditions.notes: Do not freeze. Do not use if solution is discolored or contains particles.
storage_conditions.light: Store in original carton to protect from light
storage_conditions.temperature: 2–8°C (refrigerated, unused pens/vials); up to 21 days at room temperature (≤30°C)
storage_conditions.shelf_life: 24 months from manufacturing date; discard 21 days after first use if kept at room temp

pharmacokinetics.steady_state: Reached after 4 weeks of once-weekly dosing
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal (~51%) and fecal (~49%); parent compound undetectable in urine
pharmacokinetics.dose_proportionality: Approximately linear across 2.5–15 mg dose range
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Proteolytic degradation; C18 fatty diacid moiety extends half-life via albumin binding

mechanisms: Selective dual GIP (GIPR) and GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) agonism (twincretin mechanism)
GIP receptor agonism potentiating insulin sensitivity in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue
GLP-1 receptor agonism providing incretin effect with glucose-dependent insulin secretion
Central hypothalamic appetite suppression via GLP-1R signaling on vagal afferents and area postrema
Glucagon suppression during hyperglycemic states reducing hepatic glucose output
Delayed gastric emptying reducing post-prandial glucose excursions
GIP-mediated reduction in hepatic fat via GIPR signaling on hepatocytes
safetyNote: FDA-approved prescription medication (Mounjaro® / Zepbound®). For research use only when obtained outside approved channels. Contraindicated in patients with personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2). Boxed warning: thyroid C-cell tumors observed in rodents.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC)
Hypersensitivity to tirzepatide or any excipient
Concurrent use of other GLP-1 receptor agonists (pharmacodynamic duplication)
Type 1 diabetes mellitus (not studied; risk of diabetic ketoacidosis)
Severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease (limited data)
Pregnancy or breastfeeding (teratogenic risk in animal studies)
History of pancreatitis — use with caution; discontinue if suspected
research_status: FDA Approved (Phase III — SURPASS & SURMOUNT trials)

faqModalItems.q: Why is Tirzepatide often shown in multiple strengths?
faqModalItems.a: Because the family is commonly represented in several vial strengths, the UI should treat it as one peptide family with variant selection.

faqModalItems.a: Metabolic health, incretin signaling, body composition, appetite-related research, and insulin-response related terms.

faqModalItems.a: Yes, especially when the protocol context is weight-management, metabolic support, or incretin-pathway exploration.

faqModalItems.a: Use concise answers covering mechanism, strengths, protocol relevance, and literature access rather than commercial copy.

faqModalItems.a: No. Scientific list views should emphasize evidence, mechanism, and strengths first, leaving pricing to the PDP if needed.

storage_conditions.temperature: 2–8°C (refrigerated, unused pens/vials); up to 21 days at room temperature (≤30°C)
storage_conditions.shelf_life: 24 months from manufacturing date; discard 21 days after first use if kept at room temp

pharmacokinetics.steady_state: Reached after 4 weeks of once-weekly dosing
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal (~51%) and fecal (~49%); parent compound undetectable in urine
pharmacokinetics.dose_proportionality: Approximately linear across 2.5–15 mg dose range
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Proteolytic degradation; C18 fatty diacid moiety extends half-life via albumin binding

mechanisms: Selective dual GIP (GIPR) and GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) agonism (twincretin mechanism)
GIP receptor agonism potentiating insulin sensitivity in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue
GLP-1 receptor agonism providing incretin effect with glucose-dependent insulin secretion
Central hypothalamic appetite suppression via GLP-1R signaling on vagal afferents and area postrema
Glucagon suppression during hyperglycemic states reducing hepatic glucose output
Delayed gastric emptying reducing post-prandial glucose excursions
GIP-mediated reduction in hepatic fat via GIPR signaling on hepatocytes
safetyNote: FDA-approved prescription medication (Mounjaro® / Zepbound®). For research use only when obtained outside approved channels. Contraindicated in patients with personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2). Boxed warning: thyroid C-cell tumors observed in rodents.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC)
Hypersensitivity to tirzepatide or any excipient
Concurrent use of other GLP-1 receptor agonists (pharmacodynamic duplication)
Type 1 diabetes mellitus (not studied; risk of diabetic ketoacidosis)
Severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease (limited data)
Pregnancy or breastfeeding (teratogenic risk in animal studies)
History of pancreatitis — use with caution; discontinue if suspected
research_status: FDA Approved (Phase III — SURPASS & SURMOUNT trials)

Tirzepatide once weekly for the treatment of obesity in people with type 2 diabetes (SURMOUNT-2): a double-blind, randomised, multicentre, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial.
